claim: A scared or skittish lost dog signals its state through averted gaze, flattened ears, tucked tail, cowering, and a nervous gait — distinguishing it from an approachable dog.
stance_target: behavioral indicators for distinguishing evasion mode from approachable state
scope_conditions: Visual assessment of found/sighted dog from safe distance
quoted_support: According to pet detective Kat Albrecht, a lost, scared, or shy dog may avert their gaze, flatten their ears, tuck their tail, cower, or walk and run in a way that looks nervous and unsure.

claim: When approaching a scared lost dog, rescuers must not approach quickly, make loud noises, call out to the dog, or make direct eye contact.
stance_target: never-chase and never-call rules; calming approach protocol
scope_conditions: First contact with scared/shy lost dog
quoted_support: Don't approach the dog too quickly. Don't make loud noises or call out to the dog. Remain calm and use submissive body language. Avoid direct eye contact.

claim: Scared lost dogs predictably shelter at the edges of communities, on hiking trail margins, in tall grass, cemeteries, golf courses, campgrounds, and industrial parks — not in central populated areas.
stance_target: search location priority for dogs in evasion mode
scope_conditions: Urban and peri-urban search areas; shy or scared dogs
quoted_support: If a dog is scared or shy, Kathy Pobloskie of Lost Dogs of America says that you can often find them on hiking trails, slightly off the beaten path (they may have sore feet from running), and they will often hide in areas like: Houses that back onto wooded areas or parks, Tall grass or marshy areas, Cemeteries, Golf courses, Campgrounds and picnic areas, Sporting fields, Industrial parks and abandoned factories.

claim: Large volunteer search parties actively searching for a scared dog will pressure it out of its safe area or into traffic, making recovery less likely.
stance_target: counter-intuitive restraint on search party size for shy/scared dogs
quoted_support: 'We understand that people want to help, but their efforts should be used for flyering, not searching,' Pobloskie says. 'Don't be the reason a dog is pressured out of their safe area, or worse yet, into traffic.'

claim: A failed humane trapping attempt reduces the probability of future successful trapping and risks injuring the dog, making proper trap selection, placement, baiting, and monitoring critical.
stance_target: humane trapping as a last-resort precision technique, not a default
scope_conditions: Trapping phase for skittish/evasive dogs that resist direct approach
quoted_support: 'You want to select the proper location, set the trap up correctly, and bait it properly,' says Danielle Robertson of Lost Pet Research & Recovery. 'Most importantly, you want to make sure that you monitor the trap adequately.' Failure to take these critical steps can reduce the chances of catching a dog, and worst case could result in seriously injuring the pet.

claim: Safety advice from animal control: provide an accurate location, direction of travel, and description of the sighted dog to responders rather than attempting personal capture.
stance_target: observer restraint — reporting over chasing as default public response to sighting
quoted_support: 'Sometimes the best help you can provide is an accurate location, the direction of travel, and description of the lost pet so responders can quickly contain them,' says Amy-Jo Sites, director for Fort Wayne Animal Care & Control.
